reports.title: No Evidence Available for This Edition
reports.tldr: No RSS entries or search snippets were provided for the April 25, 2026 edition. Without source evidence, Morning cannot responsibly report on any topics. Please check that the feed pipeline delivered content before regenerating this report.

sections.heading: What Happened?
sections.body: The evidence block supplied to the editorial agent was empty — no RSS entries, no search snippets, and no supplementary data arrived for this edition. Morning's editorial policy requires that every claim trace back to provided evidence, so rather than fabricate stories or draw on training-data knowledge, this edition is intentionally left blank. Rerun the pipeline once feeds are confirmed healthy.

reports.title: Robots That Learn, Adapt, and Explain Themselves
reports.tldr: Today's feed is unusually rich. Three complementary themes dominate: (1) making robots safer and more explainable at every level — from surgical suturing to humanoid fighting — by grounding decisions in explicit representations; (2) closing the gap between high-level language reasoning and low-level physical control in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models; and (3) building better data infrastructure and training pipelines so robots can be post-trained efficiently without constant real-world intervention. Hardware-specific research is light today; most evidence is software and systems-level.

sections.heading: Exoskeletons Get a Wrist — and It Makes a Big Difference
sections.body:
Three related papers from the same research group dropped today on upper-limb exoskeletons (wearable robotic arm supports), and together they tell a clean story. The first paper introduces a mechanical design: a single Bowden cable — essentially a bicycle brake cable — tensioned by a coiled spring to move the wrist side-to-side (abduction-adduction) without needing a motor at the joint itself. This keeps the device light and friction-free. Simulation was used to pick the right spring stiffness, and the prototype was validated on five healthy participants.

The second paper asks the practical question: does this wrist joint actually help? Eight healthy adults and one person with ALS (a progressive motor neuron disease) used the EXOTIC2 exoskeleton on a drinking task and a scratch task, with the wrist joint either active or locked out. The results are striking — spill rate during drinking dropped from 56% to 3%, and success at the scratch task jumped from 28% to 75%.

The third paper took this into a real clinical population: people with ALS and spinal cord injury (SCI). They used a tongue-controlled 6-degree-of-freedom (DoF — meaning six independent axes of movement) version of the same exoskeleton. Results held up: spillage fell from 78% to 22%, and failed placements from 67% to 17%. Critically, users didn't find the extra joint uncomfortable — they just used it as needed for the task, not to its full range of motion.

Takeaway: A single lightweight cable-driven wrist joint has outsized functional impact for people with severe motor impairments. The practical lesson is that completeness of a kinematic chain (covering the right joints) matters more than raw strength or precision.

sections.heading: VLA Models: Fixing the Gap Between Seeing, Saying, and Doing
sections.body:
Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models are AI systems that take camera images and natural language instructions as input and output robot actions directly. They have become the dominant paradigm in robotics research, and today's feed contains at least five papers trying to fix their known weaknesses.

**How VLAs Actually Fail in the Real World.** One paper conducted a systematic audit of state-of-the-art VLAs on the BEHAVIOR1K benchmark — a suite of complex household tasks. The key finding: standard success-rate metrics hide dangerous behaviors. The models often achieve partial credit by lucky final states, not by performing tasks safely or consistently. The paper proposes new evaluation protocols that track safety violations explicitly. This is an important calibration — the field may be overstating progress.

**CorridorVLA: Spatial Guardrails for Action Generation.** Standard VLAs generate actions from noise (a process called diffusion), which is flexible but doesn't naturally encode where the robot arm should go in physical space. CorridorVLA introduces sparse spatial anchors — predicted intermediate positions the action must pass through — and uses them to define a "corridor" of acceptable trajectories during training. Paths outside the corridor get corrective training signal; minor deviations due to contact or noise are tolerated. On the LIBERO-Plus benchmark, this improves success rates by 3–12 percentage points over baselines, with one variant reaching 83.2%.

**ResVLA: Decoupling Intent from Fine Motor Control.** ResVLA attacks a deeper problem: VLAs are trained to generate entire action sequences from noise, but a robot's motion naturally has two layers — the broad intent (where am I going?) and the local dynamics (how exactly do I get there?). ResVLA uses spectral analysis (a mathematical technique that separates slow, smooth signals from fast, noisy ones) to split the motion into a low-frequency "anchor" capturing intent, and a high-frequency residual for fine adjustments. The generative model then only has to refine the residual on top of a deterministic anchor. The result is faster convergence, better robustness to language variation, and competitive real-world performance.

**LoHo-Manip: Long-Horizon Planning with Visual Traces.** Most VLAs are good at short, single-step tasks. LoHo-Manip adds a separate Vision-Language Model (VLM) manager that watches the robot's progress and continuously re-plans the remaining steps, outputting both a language subtask sequence and a visual trace — a 2D keypoint path drawn on the image showing where to go next. The executor VLA follows the trace. When a step fails, it simply remains in the next plan. This implicit replanning avoids brittle hand-crafted recovery logic. Evaluated on a real Franka robot arm and in simulation, it shows strong gains on multi-step tasks.

**VistaBot: Robustness to Camera Viewpoint Changes.** A persistent weakness of VLAs trained with a fixed camera: move the camera, and performance collapses. VistaBot adds a 4D geometry estimation step (estimating the 3D structure of the scene over time) and uses a video diffusion model to synthesize what the scene would look like from the training camera's viewpoint — even at test time when the camera has moved. This view synthesis is fused into the policy's latent representation. VistaBot improves the View Generalization Score — a new metric they introduce — by 2.6–2.8x over baseline policies.

sections.heading: Medical Robotics Gets Its First Open Foundation Dataset
sections.body:
One of the most infrastructurally significant papers today introduces Open-H-Embodiment, described as the largest open dataset of medical robot video with synchronized kinematics (joint position and velocity data recorded alongside video). It spans more than 49 institutions and covers multiple surgical robots including the da Vinci, the CMR Versius, Moon Surgical Maestro, and others, across surgical manipulation, robotic ultrasound, and endoscopy.

The dataset enables two demonstrated results. GR00T-H is the first open foundation VLA model for medical robotics — a Vision-Language-Action model trained specifically on surgical data. It is the only evaluated model to complete an end-to-end suturing benchmark (25% of trials, compared to 0% for all other models tested), and achieves 64% average success across a 29-step suturing sequence.

The second system, Cosmos-H-Surgical-Simulator, is an action-conditioned world model — a model that, given an action, can predict what the scene will look like next. It supports nine different robot platforms from a single trained checkpoint, enabling synthetic data generation and in-simulation policy testing without physical robots.

This matters because medical robotics has long suffered from exactly the data fragmentation problem that held back general robotics a decade ago: small, proprietary, single-platform datasets. Open-H-Embodiment is positioning itself as the equivalent of ImageNet for surgical robotics.

sections.heading: Post-Training Without Constant Real-World Robot Time
sections.body:
A recurring frustration in robotics is that improving a deployed robot policy requires running it in the real world, which is slow, expensive, and requires a human to supervise every failure. Two papers today address this from complementary angles.

**Hi-WM: Human Corrections Inside a World Model.** Hi-WM proposes using a learned world model — a neural network that can simulate what will happen when the robot takes an action — as a substrate for human correction. Instead of physically running the robot until it fails, a human watches the robot fail inside the world model simulation, jumps in to provide a corrective action, and the system rolls out the corrected trajectory. The world model supports rollback (going back to a failure state) and branching (trying multiple corrections from the same state), yielding dense training signal from rare failures. Real-world success improved by 37.9 percentage points over the base policy on average, and world-model evaluation correlated strongly with real performance (r = 0.953 — very high). The key insight: world models aren't just for imagination or data augmentation; they can be interactive correction environments.

**RLFP: Foundation Models as Reward Designers.** The updated Reinforcement Learning with Foundation Priors (RLFP) paper (now at version 5) takes a different angle: use foundation models — large pretrained models — not to generate actions but to provide reward signals and value estimates that guide RL exploration. This eliminates the need for hand-crafted reward functions, one of RL's most time-consuming bottlenecks. On five real dexterous manipulation tasks, the system achieved 86% average success after one hour of real-time learning. In simulation, it hit 100% success on 7 out of 8 tasks in under 100,000 environment steps.

sections.heading: Humanoid Robots: Weightlessness, Fighting, and Wheel-Leg Hybrids
sections.body:
Three hardware-facing papers today push the envelope of what humanoid and hybrid robots can physically do.

**Learning Weightlessness.** When a human sits down, they don't rigidly track a trajectory — they relax certain joints and let the chair handle the rest. Existing humanoid control methods mostly ignore this, leading to stiff, unnatural contact with the environment. The "Learn Weightlessness" paper introduces a system that automatically labels moments in motion capture data when a joint should go passive (the "weightless" state), then trains a Weightlessness Mechanism (WM) that dynamically decides which joints to relax and by how much during contact-rich motions like sitting, lying down, or leaning against a wall. Tested on the Unitree G1 humanoid across chairs of varying heights and beds of varying inclinations, the approach generalizes well without task-specific tuning.

**Humanoid Fighting with Smooth Skill Transitions.** Making a humanoid robot perform a punch, a kick, and then a defensive block in sequence is hard because each motion has a different starting and ending body posture — the "seam" between skills is where falls happen. RPG (Robust Policy Gating) trains a unified policy using motion transition randomization (randomly varying the starting state of each skill during training) and temporal randomization (varying when transitions occur). The result is a system that can perform arbitrary-duration combat sequences on the Unitree G1 robot without instability at transitions.

**X2-N: Wheels and Legs in One Body.** X2-N is a robot that can operate as a humanoid (walking on legs with flat feet) or as a wheel-legged robot (rolling on wheels), and can transform between the two by reconfiguring its joints. It also has a full upper body with arms for manipulation. A reinforcement learning whole-body controller handles locomotion, transformation, and arm control in a unified framework. Validated on stair climbing, skating-like motion, and package delivery — an unusual combination that demonstrates genuine versatility.

sections.heading: LLM Agents: Skill Banks, Governance, and Long-Horizon Reasoning
sections.body:
Outside of robotics, two AI agent papers stand out.

**COSPLAY: LLM Agents That Build and Reuse Skills.** COSPLAY addresses a genuine weakness of LLM-based agents: they process each episode from scratch, without a persistent memory of what worked before. COSPLAY introduces a co-evolution loop: an LLM decision agent retrieves skills from a learnable skill bank to guide its actions; a separate skill pipeline watches the agent's trajectories and extracts reusable skill primitives from them, updating the bank. Both improve together over time. Across six game environments, an 8B-parameter base model achieves 25.1% average reward improvement over four much larger frontier LLM baselines on single-player benchmarks. The efficiency gain from structured skill reuse is substantial.

**Defensibility Index: Escaping the Agreement Trap.** Content moderation and rule-governed AI systems are typically evaluated by how often they agree with human-labeled examples. This paper argues that in rule-governed environments, this is fundamentally wrong: multiple decisions can be logically valid under a policy, so disagreement doesn't mean error. The authors introduce the Defensibility Index (DI) — measuring whether a decision is logically derivable from the governing rules — and the Probabilistic Defensibility Signal (PDS), estimated from the token probability distributions of an LLM auditor without additional inference passes. Validated on 193,000+ Reddit moderation decisions, they find a 33–47 percentage-point gap between agreement-based and policy-grounded metrics. 79–80% of apparent model errors were actually policy-valid decisions. A practical governance gate built on these signals achieves 78.6% automation coverage with 64.9% risk reduction.

sections.heading: The Common Thread: Explicit Representations as the Bridge
sections.body:
At first glance, today's papers span wildly different domains — wrist exoskeletons, surgical robots, humanoid fighting, LLM content moderation, UAV landing, and off-road driving. But there is a single idea running through nearly all of them: the value of making internal representations explicit and interpretable, rather than letting everything remain hidden inside neural network weights.

CorridorVLA makes spatial constraints explicit as trajectory corridors. ResVLA makes intent explicit as a low-frequency anchor. LoHo-Manip makes planning state explicit as a visual trace and subtask list. Hi-WM makes failure states explicit and reusable inside a world model. eSEC-LAM makes manipulation understanding explicit as symbolic predicates. The Defensibility Index makes policy reasoning explicit as a logical derivation. The wrist exoskeleton papers make a mechanical insight explicit: the missing joint was the bottleneck, and adding it explicitly changes outcomes dramatically.

The overarching trend is a maturation of the field away from pure end-to-end black-box learning toward hybrid systems that combine the pattern-matching power of large neural models with explicit structure — whether spatial, temporal, logical, or mechanical. This is not a retreat from deep learning; it is a recognition that explicit structure makes systems more data-efficient, more robust, safer, and easier to correct.

Looking forward, the natural next step is automatic discovery of what structure to make explicit — systems that learn not just from data but learn which aspects of their own behavior are worth representing symbolically or geometrically. The skill bank in COSPLAY, the weightlessness labeling in the humanoid paper, and the spectral decomposition in ResVLA are all early steps in that direction. Expect this hybrid explicit-implicit design pattern to become the dominant paradigm in embodied AI over the next two to three years.

reports.title: Medical robot foundation models, VLA policy analysis, and a surge in exoskeleton research
reports.tldr: Today's arXiv batch is unusually large and coherent. Three headline items stand out: Open-H-Embodiment introduces the largest open medical robotics dataset to date and two foundation models built on it; a critical paper dissects VLA safety and evaluation gaps in open-world benchmarks; and a cluster of exoskeleton papers provides clinical evidence that wrist abduction-adduction assistance meaningfully improves daily-living task outcomes. Humanoid control, world-model-based post-training, and improved VLA action heads round out the day.

sections.heading: Open-H-Embodiment: A foundation model push for medical robotics
sections.body: Researchers from more than 49 institutions have released Open-H-Embodiment, described as the largest open dataset of medical robotic video with synchronized kinematics. It spans seven robotic platforms including the da Vinci, Versius, MIRA, and Maestro systems, and covers surgical manipulation, robotic ultrasound, and endoscopy. Two models are demonstrated on the dataset. GR00T-H is presented as the first open vision-language-action model for medical robotics; it is the only evaluated model to achieve full end-to-end task completion on a structured suturing benchmark, completing 25% of trials versus 0% for all comparison models, and achieving 64% average success across a 29-step ex vivo suturing sequence. The second model, Cosmos-H-Surgical-Simulator, is an action-conditioned world model spanning nine robotic platforms that supports policy evaluation and synthetic data generation. The authors frame the dataset as critical research infrastructure, arguing that data fragmentation has been the primary bottleneck for foundation model development in this domain.

sections.heading: How well do VLAs actually behave? A critical look at open-world evaluation
sections.body: A new analysis paper argues that standard benchmarks for vision-language-action models — including the widely used BEHAVIOR1K challenge — measure only final object states, ignoring the sequence of actions that produced them. The authors call this a 'progress-agnostic' evaluation protocol and contend it can exaggerate reported performance and obscure safety-relevant behaviors. They evaluate state-of-the-art models on B1K and assess reproducibility, consistency, safety violations, and task awareness. Their proposed evaluation protocols explicitly capture safety violations during policy execution. The paper does not claim current VLAs are unsafe in deployed products, but identifies what it characterizes as a gap between benchmark scores and the real-world deployment requirements of complex, interactive household tasks. This is a methodological critique paper; claims about specific models are based on the authors' own experiments.

sections.heading: Wrist exoskeletons: clinical evidence accumulates
sections.body: Three related papers published together build a case for including wrist abduction-adduction (Ab-Ad) as an active degree of freedom in upper-limb exoskeletons. The first paper presents a tendon-driven, torsional-spring-assisted mechanism using a single Bowden cable, validated with five non-disabled participants. The second integrates the mechanism into a five-DoF exoskeleton (EXOTIC2) and tests it with eight non-disabled adults: spill incidence during a drinking task fell from 56% to 3% with wrist assistance enabled, and success on a scratch-leveling task improved from 28% to 75%. The third paper reports a within-subject clinical study with one ALS patient and five spinal cord injury patients using a tongue-controlled six-DoF version of EXOTIC2: spillage dropped from 77.8% to 22.2% and failed placements from 66.7% to 16.7%. Participants reported no increase in discomfort. Across all three studies the evidence is consistent: active wrist Ab-Ad control provides meaningful functional benefit without adding perceived burden, though sample sizes remain small and the clinical findings are preliminary.

sections.heading: World models as corrective substrates for robot post-training
sections.body: Hi-WM (Human-in-the-World-Model) proposes using a learned world model as the substrate for human-guided policy correction, rather than requiring physical robot execution for each intervention. A policy is rolled out inside the world model; when failures appear, a human provides short corrective actions directly in the simulation. The framework supports rollback and branching, allowing one failure state to seed multiple corrective continuations. Evaluated on three real-world manipulation tasks across two policy backbones, Hi-WM improved real-world success by 37.9 percentage points on average over the base policy and by 19.0 points over a world-model closed-loop baseline. World-model evaluation correlated strongly with real-world performance (r = 0.953). The authors position this as evidence that world models can function as corrective tools for scalable post-training, not only as generators or evaluators.

sections.heading: Improving VLA action heads: CorridorVLA and ResVLA
sections.body: Two papers propose architectural improvements to how vision-language-action models generate low-level control. CorridorVLA predicts sparse spatial anchors as incremental physical changes and uses them to define an explicit tolerance corridor in the training objective for a flow-matching action head. On the LIBERO-Plus benchmark, it improves success rate by 3.4–12.4 percentage points over SmolVLA and GR00T baselines; the GR00T-Corr variant reaches 83.21%. ResVLA takes a different approach, using spectral analysis to decompose control into a deterministic low-frequency intent and a stochastic high-frequency residual, then anchoring a residual diffusion bridge on the predicted intent. Simulation experiments show competitive performance and faster convergence than standard generative baselines, with positive real-world results also reported. Both papers address the same underlying challenge: better aligning semantic language understanding with precise physical action generation.

sections.heading: Humanoid control: weightlessness, fighting, and wheel-leg transformation
sections.body: Three humanoid robotics papers address distinct control challenges. The 'Learn Weightlessness' paper observes that humans selectively relax joints during non-self-stabilizing motions — sitting, lying down, leaning — and proposes a Weightlessness Mechanism that automatically determines which joints to relax during environment contact. Tested on the Unitree G1 robot across chair, bed, and wall scenarios, the method generalizes across varying configurations without task-specific tuning. RPG (Robust Policy Gating) targets dynamic multi-skill fighting on humanoids, using motion transition randomization and temporal randomization to train a policy that transitions smoothly between fighting skills and locomotion; real-world experiments on the Unitree G1 are reported. X2-N presents a transformable wheel-legged humanoid that can switch between wheeled and legged modes via joint reconfiguration, controlled by a reinforcement learning whole-body framework validated in skating, stair-climbing, and package delivery tasks.

sections.heading: Other signals worth noting
sections.body: Several additional papers merit brief mention. Open-H-Embodiment (covered above) also releases Cosmos-H-Surgical-Simulator as a separate contribution. The MISTY motion planner achieves single-step inference for autonomous driving trajectory generation at over 99 FPS with 10.1 ms latency, reaching state-of-the-art results on the nuPlan Test14-hard split. FingerViP embeds miniature cameras in each fingertip of a dexterous hand to overcome wrist-camera occlusion, reporting 80.8% overall success across challenging real-world tasks including retrieving objects behind an occluding curtain. A legged robotics survey specifically addresses locomotion in non-inertial environments — ships, aircraft, moving vehicles — identifying this as an underexplored area where stationary-ground assumptions break down. The COSPLAY agent framework co-evolves an LLM decision agent with a learnable skill bank for long-horizon game tasks, achieving 25.1% average reward improvement over frontier LLM baselines with an 8B model.
